title: Furnace Replacement

budget.description: 80% AFUE single-stage gas furnace, basic brand (Goodman or Amana), direct replacement with existing ductwork and venting

midRange.description: 90-95% AFUE two-stage gas furnace, mid-tier brand (Rheem, Trane, or Carrier), standard installation with smart thermostat

premium.description: 97-98% AFUE modulating variable-speed gas furnace, premium brand (Lennox or American Standard), with zoning controls and whole-house humidifier

costFactors.title: Furnace Efficiency (AFUE Rating)
costFactors.description: AFUE (Annual Fuel Utilization Efficiency) measures how much fuel becomes heat. An 80% AFUE unit wastes 20 cents of every dollar. A 95% AFUE furnace costs $800-$1,500 more upfront but saves $200-$400 per year on gas bills. Top-tier 98% AFUE modulating furnaces add another $1,000-$2,000 but offer the best long-term savings.

costFactors.title: Furnace Size (BTU Output)
costFactors.description: Furnaces are sized by BTU output. A 60,000 BTU unit heats 1,200-1,500 sq ft and costs $1,200-$2,500 for the equipment. An 80,000 BTU unit covers 1,600-2,000 sq ft at $1,500-$3,200. Large 120,000+ BTU units for 2,500+ sq ft homes run $2,200-$4,500. Proper sizing through a Manual J calculation prevents short cycling and cold spots.

costFactors.title: Venting Changes
costFactors.description: An 80% AFUE furnace uses a standard metal chimney flue. Upgrading to a 90%+ high-efficiency unit requires PVC venting through a sidewall, which costs $300-$800 for the venting work. If your chimney flue needs to be abandoned and capped, add another $200-$500.

costFactors.title: Ductwork Condition
costFactors.description: If your existing ductwork is undersized, leaky, or damaged, the new furnace will not perform efficiently. Duct sealing runs $500-$1,200. Partial duct replacement costs $1,000-$2,500. A complete ductwork overhaul for a 2,000 sq ft home runs $3,000-$7,000.

costFactors.title: Fuel Type Conversion
costFactors.description: Switching from oil to gas requires a new gas line ($500-$2,000) plus the furnace swap. Converting from electric to gas involves gas line installation and new venting. Switching from gas to electric (heat pump) may need electrical panel upgrades. Staying with your current fuel type is always the cheapest path.

materialOptions.pros: Lowest upfront cost, simple and reliable, fewer parts to fail
materialOptions.cons: Highest operating costs, louder operation, less even heating
materialOptions.bestFor: Mild climates, budget-focused homeowners, homes with high ceilings or poor insulation where efficiency gains are minimal

materialOptions.pros: Good balance of cost and efficiency, quieter on low stage, more even temperatures
materialOptions.cons: Higher upfront cost, requires PVC venting
materialOptions.bestFor: Most homeowners in cold climates, the sweet spot of cost vs. performance

materialOptions.pros: Lowest operating costs, whisper-quiet, ultra-even heating, best humidity control
materialOptions.cons: Highest upfront cost, more complex repairs, longer payback period
materialOptions.bestFor: Cold climates with high heating bills, homeowners planning to stay 10+ years

materialOptions.pros: Produces very hot air, works in areas without natural gas
materialOptions.cons: Higher fuel costs, requires oil storage tank, more maintenance
materialOptions.bestFor: Rural areas without natural gas service, Northeast homes with existing oil infrastructure

materialOptions.pros: Lowest upfront cost, no combustion or venting, 100% efficient at point of use
materialOptions.cons: Very high operating costs in most areas, slow to heat
materialOptions.bestFor: Mild climates with low heating needs, areas with very cheap electricity, supplemental heating

northeast.label: Northeast
northeast.note: High labor rates and strict code requirements. Oil-to-gas conversions are common and add cost.

westCoast.label: West Coast
westCoast.note: California and Washington pushing heat pump mandates; gas furnace installs face additional regulatory costs

southeast.label: Southeast
southeast.note: Lower heating demand means smaller units and simpler installs. Labor rates are below average.

midwest.label: Midwest
midwest.note: Competitive HVAC market keeps prices down despite high heating demand. Gas is readily available.

mountainWest.label: Mountain West
mountainWest.note: Altitude affects combustion ratings - units may need high-altitude kits adding $100-$300

diyVsPro.diyFeasibility: Not recommended - furnace replacement involves gas lines, electrical wiring, and combustion venting that require licensed professionals in most jurisdictions
diyTasks: Researching and purchasing the furnace unit directly
Replacing air filters and basic thermostat swaps
Removing the old furnace after professional disconnection
proOnlyTasks: Gas line connections and leak testing
Electrical wiring and control board hookups
Venting installation (both standard and high-efficiency PVC)
Permit pulling, inspections, and code compliance
Refrigerant line work if combined with AC
diyVsPro.diyRisks: Gas furnace installation errors can cause carbon monoxide poisoning, gas leaks, house fires, and explosions. Most jurisdictions require licensed HVAC technicians and permits. DIY installation voids virtually all manufacturer warranties and may void your homeowner's insurance.

savingsTips: Get quotes in spring or early fall when HVAC companies are less busy - many offer 10-20% off-season discounts
Look for manufacturer rebates, which typically run $200-$800 and stack with utility rebates
Check your utility company for efficiency rebates - many offer $300-$1,000 for high-AFUE units
Consider buying the furnace unit directly and hiring an HVAC company for install only to save 15-25%
Choose a two-stage 92-95% AFUE unit for the best cost-to-savings ratio in cold climates
Bundle with AC replacement - HVAC companies often discount 10-15% when replacing both systems
Ask about financing - many manufacturers offer 0% APR for 12-36 months through dealer programs
Skip the extended warranty if your installer offers a workmanship guarantee - manufacturer warranties cover 10-20 years on heat exchangers

contractorQuestions.question: What size furnace does my home need, and how did you determine that?
contractorQuestions.why: A proper Manual J load calculation accounts for your home's square footage, insulation, windows, and climate. A contractor who just matches your old furnace size may over- or under-size the replacement.

contractorQuestions.question: What AFUE rating do you recommend, and what is the payback period?
contractorQuestions.why: The most efficient furnace is not always the best value. A good contractor will calculate your estimated annual savings and show you the payback timeline.

contractorQuestions.question: Will my existing ductwork and venting work with the new unit?
contractorQuestions.why: Upgrading from 80% to 90%+ AFUE requires PVC venting instead of a chimney flue. Undersized ductwork reduces efficiency and comfort regardless of furnace quality.

contractorQuestions.question: Is the quote all-inclusive - unit, labor, materials, permits, disposal, thermostat?
contractorQuestions.why: HVAC quotes vary wildly in what they include. Some bundle the thermostat and permits; others charge separately for everything.

contractorQuestions.question: What brand and model are you quoting, and what is the warranty?
contractorQuestions.why: Generic 'high-efficiency furnace' quotes make it impossible to compare. Get the exact model number so you can verify specifications and pricing.

contractorQuestions.question: Are you licensed and insured, and will you pull the permit?
contractorQuestions.why: Unlicensed HVAC work is illegal in most states. Unpermitted installations can cause problems at home sale and may void insurance coverage.

contractorQuestions.question: Do you offer a workmanship warranty beyond the manufacturer warranty?
contractorQuestions.why: Manufacturer warranties cover the equipment but not the installation labor. A 1-2 year workmanship guarantee protects you from installation defects.

secondaryKeywords: how much does a furnace cost
seo.metaDescription: Furnace replacement costs $2,500-$7,500 in 2026, with a national average of $4,500. See detailed breakdowns by efficiency, size, and brand plus use our interactive calculator.

sources.name: Angi - How Much Does a New Furnace Cost?
U.S. Department of Energy - Furnaces and Boilers
